author: @alext8828
text:
I can see already that the 4 guide vanes are oriented badly.  In an effort to capitalize on the left-hand incoming breeze and nullifying the right hand (reverse rotation) breeze, the guide vanes should be placed on a rather more tangential position to the circumferential rim on the rotor, thus blocking the breeze on the right (as you face the rotor) and causing the breeze to be "pinched between the left vane and the rotor blades.  This will perhaps increase the venturi effect and turn the rotor more briskly.  This, of course, can be tuned up by making fine adjustments to the vanes.   Great looking machine.
I need to edit in that your blower is only on the left side of a vane and the wind will be blowing on the entire effective projected area of the rotor.  I may even suggest the rotor blades are in backward, but I'm not entirely positive they are.

author: @NorthernKitty
text:
Waiting around until we find the "perfect" material or method actually stifles creativity.  Conversely, placing constraints before rolling up our sleeves inspires creativity.  When we're forced to work with limitations, we'll work harder to think on a better approach.  The famous Dr. Seuss book "Green Eggs and Ham" was the result of a bet the author had that he could write a children's book using only 50 words.
It's one thing I immediately loved about this series when I saw the title:  "A 1kW wind generator for $100".  Wonderful!  A constraint!!!  It's akin to the jest I've made before, "let's drop Rob in a scrap yard for an hour and see what he can build."  It's fascinating watching your creative juices flow to meet such a constraint/challenge.  I knew right away I was going to love this series, and I haven't been disappointed.  😄😄😄

author: @KentuckyFriedFixes
text: Robert, another great video. Thank you. I was wondering if you have considered using the flywheel and stator from a riding lawn tractor engine on your windmill? Depending on availability of course. Here in the US it seems like old non-working engines from riding mowers are a dime a dozen but I have no idea if the same applies to engine scrap in the UK. It would be possible to mount one flywheel/stator combination at the top and one at the bottom. Or left side/right side depending how you plan to permanently mount it.  Since your windmill could be running 2, they could be wired in series to increase low RPM voltage. Another possible benefit would be the weight of the flywheels. Though more difficult to get the windmill started, once it begins to turn the weight of the flywheels would store some energy to keep it turning during any brief lulls of wind.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
Gaps between magnets is pure rubbish because as any magnet passe in very close proximity to a ferromagnetic material, a certain portion of the field automatically 'extends out' through the metallic core, because the metallic core, as long as it is not diamagnetic, is more easily passed through than air.

This means, in effect, the magnetic field is 'DRAWN' to the metal, or, perhaps, the metal is drawn to the magnetic field, but can not move, still, it is like the lightning rod effect wherein electricity always seeks out the path of least resistance.

Since this is always true, having 'gaps' in the magnetic layout is really quite irrelevant, so long as you keep your magnets as close as possible to the cores and coils.

The net result is a paramagnetic field then sympathetically [or some might say 'reactively', just depends on your attitude about it I suspect], builds up a counter-field, which, when it collapses, drives electrons through the wire, perpendicular to the travel of the magnets.

Having a very large core, very closely spaced to the coils and the magnets will give much more room for the magnetic fields to flow far wider and therefore with more of something one might have to maybe name some day, but for lack of any known official name, I shall call it 'magnetic  amperage', and therefore, the coils get more saturated before discharging.

That said, yes, of course, and quite obviously, there is a practical limit to this, AND the clear and evident laws of diminishing returns are still fully in force, however, if you can make your own field core, it is actually best for the core its self to literally be one single piece, with all of the coils neatly arranged radially around it like spokes on a wheel.

This 'endless' core, along with 'gapless' [or gapped, if you insist] magnets, will produce very strong coil saturation and produce the greatest amount of power for the strength of your magnet.

The ability for poles to create sympathetic WAVES along the whole of the core, especially if you can fine tune the speed and diameter of those waves, will to a slight extent, amplify the effective force value of your magnets, the same way the fields inside a normal AC induction motor relies on a continuously spinning wave of magnetism to reach peak efficiency.

All in all, it is most likely there will be multiple speeds like this, where the load will increase and yet the power will also increase, if you have some kind of oscilloscope, a tachometer or some other way to monitor the speed and frequency, you will likely be able to find those ideal 'sweet-spot' speeds, and work to make various adjustments to your air ducting systems to try and divert or deflect air so that the rotor spins around one of those ideal speeds.

This is what happens inside large wind mill type generation systems, and each generator is carefully tested on a very sophisticated dynometer arrangement at the factory then software is modified and fine tuned so as to maximize the time the generator's armature speed will be within that particular 'sweet spot' for that specific generator.

Interestingly, despite all of the 'machine precision' and duplicability of modern mass produced items, no 2 generators, no 2 coils, no 2 magnets, are ever going to be exactly the same, assuming you used the very precise machine to make all of them, and the only way you could match them up would be carefully winding or unwinding, adding metal, grinding metal away, pushing a magnet closer, or pulling it away until each and every one of them could then somehow be brought into conformity.

Its way too much work, goes back to 'the law of diminishing returns'.

Just get them all as close as you can, and be happy if they are all within 3% of each other!

       /)/) 
    (  'ˬ') 
@(>_ )-b 
     \) \)

author: @sceptic33
text: did you ever look into the Bedini motor circuit? i saw it used on a generator coil one time in a way that meant a switch (transistor) between then coil and storage battery was open most of the time, so high voltage built up on the coil as no current could run.. the transistor then closed for a microsecond as the magnet passed allowing a high voltage  impulse to hit the battery.. might be fun to play with if you want hv output..

author: @highlandtownshiphomerule5668
text: Wonderful videos. I have a comment about magnet spacing. To get the maximum efficiency the magnet spacing needs to match the particular core that you are using. When using a "E" core the magnets need to line up with the core. North on the ends and south in the center. Then as the unit turns then you have south on both ends and north in the center. If you don't align the magnets up then the magnet lines of force will cancel each other out. On a "C" core, you must have a north on one end and a south on the other. With a single core (bolt through a coil) the spacing is not that critical, however you will loose efficiency because you are using air to return one side of the lines of force which is not as efficient as iron. You reference a stepper motor using one magnet, however if you look closer at the magnetic field of the magnet you will see that it is magnetized with many different poles around the magnet that matches the iron core of the rotor. You reference the distance of the magnet to be very critical for power generation, you are correct. The magnet spacing is just as critical.

author: @mwm2929
text: You can use a transformer to adjust the output easily with high frequency AC.  It is just a matter of using a higher wattage coil to generate.  Think about what we do with mains voltage (line voltage in the US)  We are constantly using devices that have transformers to reduce the voltage..  High speed, high voltage generators are good when there is a lot of inertia to give you a flywheel effect of output stabilization.  That certainly keeps the electronics happy with a smooth rise and fall of output vs. jumping around constantly!

author: @SprocketN
text:
I am enjoying your videos, thank you for taking the time to produce them, however I have an observation which leads to a suggestion:
You refer to the output of your electrical generation devices in terms of volts or amps. A volt or an amp in isolation is not enough information to identify whether a device is producing sufficient power to determine if it is an idea is worth pursuing using Watts makes this comparison much easier.
For example, by identifying the power output of one coil in terms of Watts it would be easy to show that multiple coils would produce more power as the total Wattage of the whole device has increased. Remembering of course that the increased power out means more power needs to go in to the system in terms of the wind energy. The additional coils will effectively cause additional drag on the turbine.
Your wind turbine, is a wonderful thing, the mechanical aspects are well thought out and well executed and I’ve no doubt it will generate power, however with you only talking in terms of amps or volts we get no idea of the potential power of the system. Please use Watts.

Whilst typing this I have thought of a question:
Since the batteries produce only around 5v how will you overcome the volt drop in the wiring from your roof to the point of use of the electrical load if you charge each group independently?

author: @radioroestig9638
text:
As far as i understand the lectures at my university, the limiting factor in producing power  here is changing the magnetic flux inside the transformer (although it kind of functions like a futuristic LP pickup), as fast as possible, and close to magnetic saturation of the transformer core. It is all just a fun game of optimizing, indeed there is no need to use expensive magnets, if the distance between the magnets and the transformer wobbles up and down a whole centimeter! 

I love this channel by the way, because you show the whole process, and not just the finished product. Sadly this type of content is exceedingly rare on youtube.

author: @buccleuch7621
text:
As a young man I had the fortunate opportunity of 'messing about on boats' with a number of ex-navy men. When things  didn't go to plan, we would sit down and convene. Inevitably, someone would say: "why don't we . . .? "
I am a bodger to this day and have invariably found the 'why don't we. . . ? ' approach produces a satisfactory solution.
Why don't we stop striving for perfection and just secure a working solution?  The world would be a much more productive space. Nuff said.
Keep up the great work Robert and ignore the naysayers!

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Coil but more importantly magnetic pole arrangement does matter a lot. If you have the E shape when a north side magnet is at the center a south side should be at the other poles. If you have the C shape when a north side is on the left pole a south side should be on the right pole. Having the single bolt through I coil will work but will produce significantly less power.
For what charging batteries is concerned what you actually need is an electronic controller which can do the step up and step down conversion to charge the batteries with any windspeed. If you use for example a simple series resistor to limit the current you will not get enough voltage when wind speed is low and to much current when windspeed is high. Some electronics is needed to get the most out if it.
What you are holding up in this video is not a stepper motor. It's a synchronous motor, it cannot step one step at a time. These were typically used in laserprinters to have paper advance at an exact speed. I bet you took this out of an old laserprinter ?

author: @robinhood4640
text: i can't really see the importance for the "squareness". Perfectly square is obviously better, but it's rigidity is far more important than it being square. If the thing is out of square but perfectly rigid, the distances may be different but they will all be constant, independant adjustment of each coil can be far more precise than if it is perfectly square with a bit of play.

author: @nigelwilliams7920
text: Make sure the coils are strongly attached as at rated power the drag on them will be considerable, and failure of the mount at high rpm would result in a very messy RUD (rapid unplanned disassembly).

author: @nigelwilliams7920
text: Very true sir!  Your coil arrangement is simply ‘what you have that extracts the required power’. Anything that works will do.  As discussed, a way to match power extracted to rpm will be useful else the coils will either allow over rotation ( not extracting enough power) or under rotation ( stall) - both inefficient.

author: @jdhtyler
text:
Thanks for explaining all the various ways you might solve the coil issue use what you have got approach. I like the idea of charging individual batteries.    
Years ago my father made the steelwork for wind generation near Ribblehead in Yorkshire the farmer lit his farm with car headlights and stored capacity in banks of old car batteries.
The Problem he had was that people complained that he left his lights during the day :-) he did have a good reason because his batteries would BOIL. Back in the early 1980s there was little knowledge electronic power management, if you wanted to know something new back then you went to the library.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
Output from coils will be AC... At a frequency related to rate magnets pass over coils.

If charging batteries... Output wil need to be rectified... Diodes are the usual way of doing such. diodes vary in how quickly the can turn on and off. Diodes which are quicker, better a blocking reverse current, have less voltage drop across them etc tend to be poorer at handling current. 
Maybe worthwhile considing active rectification using mosfets?

The change of output frequency offers a potential way of limiting rpm... High pass filter into a big electrical load. The load will then demand a higher power from the turbine... if that power greater than provided by wind, turbine will slow. 
In theory could use a high pass directly, but component sizes likely massive. Using output of high pass filter to trigger a n-channel mosfet to conduct down to ground likely to be far more practical.

Alternatively rather than an electrical load... A mechanical load like a band brake activated by an electromechanical solenoid.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
Annother perhaps simpler rpm limiter I've heard of is non-Newtonian grease...
Slippy at Low rpm, then becomes viscous at high rpm. Sometimes used as a simple rev limiter for rubber band powered models. 
Stuff like oublec, corn flour starch mixes... Problem with them is they tend to go mouldy after a week or so. 

A more long term mix I've heard of is that of a non volatile liquid eg glycerin, and silica flour.

author: @barbufodor1186
text: I think some of us are misinterpreting your effort.  Yes in some circumstances you can get a better outcome by optimizing some of the parameters(or materials) but often, there is a cost associated with that optimization.  I think your point is (and this is beside the pedagogic effort) a kind of proof of concept (with minimal or no financial resources)   and from there on, we can choose to march on the idea or not. Of course I can start complaining that this is trivial or this is not enough or that you are doing this for monetary gain or you should doing differently or God knows what? .... but that's only because I had sour grapes for lunch.

author: @stevecummins324
text: It's rigidity that fixes length of air gap to coil. Drum's axis could be off perpendicular by say 45deg...  OK  drum would be closer to base one side than opposite side... But unless there is wobble, those positions won't move, and gap length above fixed points on base wouldn't  change. Just use shims/a block to adjust coil height. But yes being square avoids such complexity.

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: FYI mate, that continues magnet that you were looking at is actually not continues. I'm glad you had that apart so now I can show you. Take a small magnet and try to slide it around the inside of that magnet, you will see it pull and push. What the big companies are doing now a days is awesome, They can program a magnet where it can have north south north south and so on, but they are doing it all on one side of the magnet. The amount of programming they can do to magnets now is endless combos. LOL, just thought you would like to know that in case you didn't. You are right, the spacing is not critical but is does help the overall smoothness, but on something as large as what you have here, I believe you will be fine the way you already have them, I did notice the spacing wasn't something you were looking at but it would only matter a little on power generation. As for speed, you are very correct in saying most of the time it will be turning slow but when those big storms come by, that thing you have is going to be zooming in the wind, because it was very smooth bearings on it. I love it so far. I think it will do real well and you are right, it is a low cost to create. That is very important.

replies.author: @jasonwitt8619
replies.text: I know how they are making them. What they do is they use a 3D printer setup with a magnetic tip on the end and touches the magnet in certain spots and changes the field in the spot that it touches. It creates a strong electric current in those spots just like a welder would do.

replies.author: @michaelinamoment
replies.text: @chrispollard6568 Maxwell's equations have been known for a long time. All their implications are not. The end "answers" are about how best to use them to solve a particular issue. "best" is situational as a challenge presents unique environment, budgets, and design goals. Don't over simplify to thinking you know what's best for everyone under these terms.

replies.author: @michaelinamoment
replies.text: @chrispollard6568 you are making an argument about a design goal with specific efficiency without considering other design goals. It's very silly to continue on in this discussion. A suboptimal solution in an ideal environment maybe a better solution in a suboptimal environment.

replies.author: @michaelinamoment
replies.text: @chrispollard6568 I find your claim to be very shallow true or not for reasons I already stated. Designing a solution from scrap is entirely different from enterprise deployments.
